coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of the anti-viral efficacy of three different dsRNA nanoparticles against potato virus Y using various delivery methods
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.

coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiang
Yu, Tingting
Zhang, Daoshun
Song, Hongping
Huang, Kun
Wang, Yong
Shen, Lili
Li, Ying
Wang, Fenglong
Zhang, Songbai
Jiao, Yubing
Yang, Jinguang
coredata.dc:description: Nanoparticles (NPs) derived from RNA interference (RNAi) are considered a potentially revolutionary technique in the field of plant protection in the future. However, the application of NPs in RNAi is hindered by the conflict between the high cost of RNA production and the large quantity of materials required for field application. This study aimed to evaluate the antiviral efficacy of commercially available nanomaterials, such as chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (CQAS), amine functionalized silica nano powder (ASNP), and carbon quantum dots (CQD), that carried double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) via various delivery methods, including infiltration, spraying, and root soaking. ASNP-dsRNA NPs are recommended for root soaking, which is considered the most effective method of antiviral compound application. The most effective antiviral compound tested was CQAS-dsRNA NPs delivered by root soaking. Using fluorescence, FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3, and CQD-dsCP-Cy3 NPs demonstrated the uptake and transport pathways of dsRNA NPs in plants when applied to plants in different modes. The duration of protection with NPs applied in various modes was then compared, providing references for evaluating the retention period of various types of NPs. All three types of NPs effectively silenced genes in plants and afforded at least 14 days of protection against viral infection. Particularly, CQD-dsRNA NPs could protect systemic leaves for 21 days following spraying.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (CQAS)
Amine functionalized silica nano powder (ASNP)
Carbon quantum dots (CQD)
RNAi
Potato virus Y (PVY)
DsRNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface functionalization of Mesoporous Silica and CQD
Synthesis and characterization of CQAS-dsCP NPs
Synthesis of FITC-CQAS-dsRNA-Cy3 and CQD-dsRNA-Cy3 NPs
ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP NPs uptake and transportation in plants after infiltration spraying, or root soaking
Preparation and observation of the protoplasts
Quantitative real-time PCR and western blotting
The retention period of ASNP, CQD, and CQAS-dsCP NPs treated in different modes
Selection of PVY target sequences and prokaryotic expression of dsRNA
Comparison of the target gene silencing efficiencies of CQAS-dsCP and CQAS-dsRNA mixed
Characterization of the loading capability of NPs
Cellular uptake and distribution of fluorescently labeled NPs
Material- and method-dependent RNAi efficiencies enhance PVY resistance
ASNP, CQD, and CQAS that delivered dsCP in different manners produce systemic protection
In vitro expression of prokaryotes, dsRNA extraction, and the target gene silencing efficiency comparison
CQAS delivery of the dsRNA mix resulted in efficient antiviral effects in tobacco, tomato, and pepper

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of dsCP loading into ASNP, CQD, and CQAS. TEM image of dsCP-ASNP, dsCP-CQD, and dsCP-CQAS. Size distribution (D) and Zeta potential (E) of dsCP-ASNP, dsCP-CQD, and dsCP-CQAS. F. Loading of dsCP at the ASNP mass ratios of 100:1, 60:1, 50:1, 40:1, 30:1, 20:1, and 10:1, corresponding to lanes 1–7, respectively. Complete loading was achieved at a dsRNA–ASNP mass ratio of 20:1 (lane 6). Loading of dsCP at the CQD mass ratios of 1:10, 1:30, 1:50, 1:70, 1:100, 1:150, and 1:200, corresponding to lanes 1–7, respectively. Complete loading was achieved at a dsRNA–ASNP mass ratio of 1:70 (lane 6). Loading of dsCP at the CQAS mass ratios of 1:1, 1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 1:8, 1:10, and 1:20 corresponding to lanes 1–7, respectively, Complete loading was achieved at a dsRNA–ASNP mass ratio of 1:10 (lane 1). The materials-bound dsRNA does not migrate and can be observed as fluorescence in the well (indicated by arrows). ASNP, amine functionalized silica nano powder; CQAD, chitosan quaternary ammonium salt; CQD, carbon quantum dots; dsCP, double-stranded coat protein; dsRNA, double-stranded RNA; TEM, transmission electron microscopy.

Confocal microscopy of leaf cells and stem cross-sections was performed using different methods of applying Cy3-CQAS-dsRNA-FITC to plants for 24 h. The fluorescence in the FITC (green) channel indicates the localization of FITC-CQAS. The fluorescence in the TRITC (red) channel shows the labeling of Cy3-dsCP.
Confocal micrographs of roots after applying Cy3-CQAS-dsRNA-FITC to plants for 4 h using the root immersion method (A) and fluorescence photographs of leaves protoplasts NPs (B).

The relative levels of expression of PVY CP mRNA and CP protein after infiltration with ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP (A, spraying (B), and root soaking (C) in the systemic leaves of N. benthamiana at 6 dpi. The relative levels of expression of the PVY CP mRNA and the expression of CP protein treated with ASNP-dsCP (D) infiltration, spraying, and root soaking, CQD-dsCP (E) Infiltration, spraying, and root soaking, CQAS-dsCP (F) Infiltration, spraying, and root soaking in the systemic leaves of N. benthamiana at 6 dpi. The mean values (n = 3) are represented. An analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed for incidence using Duncan's multiple range test, with * * indicating extremely significant levels.

Topical application of CQAS-dsRNA, CQD-dsRNA, and ASNP-dsRNA provided protection to N. benthamiana through three different NP application methods. The expression of PVY CP genes in plant leaves on days 7, 14, 21 after treatment with CQAS-dsRNA (A), CQD-dsRNA (B) and ASNP-dsRNA (C). Mean values (n = 3) are represented. Standard errors are represented by bars. Significant differences (P ≤ 0.05) among treatments are indicated by different letters, according to parametric oneway ANOVA.

Construction of a prokaryotic expression system and a process for product extraction. A. Gene fragment after digestion by Bgl II and Kpn I, the L4440 vector fragment, and double digestion verification of the recombinant vector. B. Construction scheme of the recombinant vector and dsRNA production. Biosynthesis of the dsRNA corresponding to a partial sequence of P1 (500 bp, Hc-Pro-1 (520 bp), Hc-Pro-2 (516 bp), Vpg-NIa (520 bp), NIa (511 bp), and CP (513 bp) genes in the E. coli HT115 (DE3). C. After the dsRNA was extracted from the bacterial solution, the bacterial genomic DNA and ssRNA were removed by treatment with DNaseI and RNaseA for 30 min. D. Expression of the target genes P1, HC-pro, VPg, NIa, and CP 6 days after virus infection in plants sprayed with CQAS-dsCP, CQAS-dsRNA, and water.

Symptoms of PVY-GFP inoculation after CQAS-dsCP and CQAS-dsRNA mixed treatments of N. benthamiana (A) and the new leaves phenotype of tomato inoculated with PVY (B). The control efficiency of a CQAS-dsRNA mixture applied by spraying tobacco, tomato, and pepper plants in the field experiment. Note: Values with the same letter show no significant differences. (LSD test, p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Evaluation of the anti-viral efficacy of three different dsRNA nanoparticles against potato virus Y using various delivery methods

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoparticles (NPs) derived from RNA interference (RNAi) are considered a potentially revolutionary technique in the field of plant protection in the future. However, the application of NPs in RNAi is hindered by the conflict between the high cost of RNA production and the large quantity of materials required for field application. This study aimed to evaluate the antiviral efficacy of commercially available nanomaterials, such as chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (CQAS), amine functionalized silica nano powder (ASNP), and carbon quantum dots (CQD), that carried double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) via various delivery methods, including infiltration, spraying, and root soaking. ASNP-dsRNA NPs are recommended for root soaking, which is considered the most effective method of antiviral compound application. The most effective antiviral compound tested was CQAS-dsRNA NPs delivered by root soaking. Using fluorescence, FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3, and CQD-dsCP-Cy3 NPs demonstrated the uptake and transport pathways of dsRNA NPs in plants when applied to plants in different modes. The duration of protection with NPs applied in various modes was then compared, providing references for evaluating the retention period of various types of NPs. All three types of NPs effectively silenced genes in plants and afforded at least 14 days of protection against viral infection. Particularly, CQD-dsRNA NPs could protect systemic leaves for 21 days following spraying.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Potato virus Y (PVY) is a positive-sense single-stranded RNA (+ssRNA) virus in the genus Potyvirus of the Potyviridae family (Bellstedt et al., 2017). PVY is one of the most significant viral infections in tobacco-producing regions around the world, resulting in substantial economic losses (Deja-Sikora et al., 2019). The detrimental effects of viral infection on host plants result in diminished performance, including a reduction in host biomass and crop yield. Currently, there is no specific treatment for PVY (Kim et al., 2017; Garces et al., 2020). Another growing issue is the development of resistance in plant pathogenic microorganisms to agricultural pesticides (Kummerer, 2004), which results in the increased introduction and use of new agricultural chemicals and higher overall costs. Consequently, RNA interference (RNAi) offers enormous potential as an effective and environmentally friendly alternative for preventing and controlling PVY (Gebremichael et al., 2021).

As a natural regulatory mechanism, RNAi serves as a defensive mechanism against transposons and viruses, and it plays an important role in animal and plant development by controlling gene expression (Van et al., 2020; Mitter et al., 2017). RNAi is an endogenous mechanism in eukaryotes that utilizes a double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) template to selectively cleave mRNAs (Fire et al., 1998). The use of dsRNA to prevent and control plant viral infections is becoming more appealing as an alternative to genetically modified (GM) crops (Mitter et al., 2017). Numerous studies have been conducted on the application of dsRNA for plant virus control. dsRNA-mediated RNAi has been demonstrated to be an effective strategy for controlling agricultural pests and diseases (X.D. Sun et al., 2020; Y. Sun et al., 2020; Fletcher et al., 2020). The main barrier to the widespread use of this technology is the development of cost-effective and reliable methods for the production and delivery of dsRNA. Furthermore, the stability of dsRNA or siRNA during and/or after administration plays a critical role in the effectiveness of RNAi technology. Although the usage of pure dsRNA can also result in gene inhibition, the effectiveness of this inhibition is quite low. This is mostly due to inadequate intracellular uptake and enzymatic degradation under natural application conditions (Lee et al., 2007). Such a limited stability period of the nucleic acids will not produce a sufficient RNAi response unless a significant quantity of dsRNA or siRNA is administered (Rego-Machado et al., 2020). Developing a reliable delivery method that enhances the stability and cellular uptake of dsRNA remains, nevertheless, the largest barrier to the widespread application of RNAi technology.

RNAi-based engineered resistance is a process that triggers RNAi by integrating pathogen gene sequences into the host genome to express specific dsRNAs or sRNAs. Spray-induced gene silencing (SIGS), which has arisen in recent years, employs only local or external application of dsRNA to protect plants from attack without incorporation into the host genome and thus avoids the issue of GM regulation (Wang and Jin, 2017; Taning et al., 2021). A promising alternative method for the control of plant diseases and pests has recently been identified as NPs-mediated RNAi. As an illustrative example, RNAi molecules have been delivered to plants via single-walled carbon nanotubes and layered hydroxide clay nanosheets, respectively (Bao et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2012); dsRNA has been combined with mesoporous silica nanoparticles to promote anti-tumor activities against breast cancer (Ultimo et al., 2016); and siRNA-loaded carbon dots have been delivered to tobacco and tomato (Schwartz et al., 2020). To inhibit gene expression, a variety of cationic polymers and lipids have been employed to produce nanoparticles. The development of nanoscale complexes increases the extent of intracellular uptake, improves nuclease stability, and confers cellular specificity when the targeting moiety is coupled to the complex. According to several studies, chitosan and its derivatives cause systemic acquired resistance (SAR) in plants (Rendina et al., 2019). In addition, a novel amine-functionalized silica nano powder (ASNP) was developed for dsRNA adsorption. ASNP, another nontoxic and environmentally acceptable delivery method can bind to and transport nucleic acids (Das et al., 2015). Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have more applications than other fluorescent nanomaterials due to their unique properties, which include neutrality, nontoxicity, simple marking, and small sizes. Positively charged CQD has been investigated as a potential dsRNA delivery vehicle in previous studies (Wang et al., 2020). The relative efficiency of dsRNA delivery to plant cells using ASNP, chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (CQAS), and CQD has not been reported in the literature. When employing ASNP, CQAS, and CQD as cell-specific gene carriers, it is highly fascinating to compare the gene silencing efficacy between various delivery mechanisms.

In this study, the efficiency of dsRNA delivery methods mediated by three NPs for silencing PVY coat protein (CP) genes were evaluated. NP-mediated delivery strategies can enhance the stability of dsRNA and improve its uptake and release from endosomes to effectively silence target genes. The efficacy of three NPs-mediated dsRNA delivery strategies in controlling PVY infestations was investigated using RNAi technology. Furthermore, fluorescent labeling and short RNA sequencing were utilized to monitor the release of NPs in plants, confirming that dsRNA encased in NPs could enter plants to induce RNAi without causing significant changes in the endogenous microRNA (miRNA) of plants. The findings of this work provide a crucial foundation for developing a safe and highly effective nanomaterial-based antiviral pesticide delivery system.

N. benthamiana plants were grown to the four-leaf stage in soil or Hoagland nutrient solution. The seedlings used for root soaking were cultured in Hoagland nutrient solution, which was changed every three days to the same stage. The plants were cultivated in a growth chamber with day/night temperatures of 25/22 °C and light/dark cycles of 16/8 h. The photosynthetic effective radiation was 100 μmol·m−2·s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functionalization of Mesoporous Silica and CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To functionalize the amine of mesoporous silica (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), 0.5 g of powdered mesoporous silica was dispersed in DMSO for 15 min using ultrasonication. After adding 400 µL of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTEOS) to the dispersion, the mixture was refluxed at 120 °C for 3 h. The product was recovered and then washed multiple times with DMSO and ethanol. After drying the particles at 60 °C, ASNP was produced.

The positive charge modification of CQD was conducted using the microwave method 24. 30 mg of carbon quantum dots were added to 9 mL of polyethylene glycol (PEG)− 200, 3 mL of H2O, and 2 mL of a 50 mg/mL polyethylenimine (PEI) aqueous solution before being microwaved at 800 W for 3 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQAS-dsCP NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of dsCP. The CP gene was amplified using the primer pairs PVYCP-F/PVYCP-R (Table S1) and dsRNA was synthesized using an in vitro Transcription T7 Kit (D6140, TaKaRa, Japan). To remove the DNA template, RNase-free DNase was spiked into the dsRNA product. The integrity of dsRNA was determined by electrophoresis of the product of enzymatic digestion, and the concentration of dsRNA was measured.

CQAS-dsCP complexation Assay. 75% deacetylated CQAS was dissolved in 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer to produce a solution with a concentration of 0.02% (w/v). The complex binding of dsCP with CQAS NPs was examined by adding drops of varying amounts of dsCP to solutions containing 0.02% CQAS (CQAS: dsCP mass ratios of 1:1, 1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 1:8, 1:10 and 1:20) in 100 µL of Na2SO4 (0.25 mol/L). Immediately following 1 min of incubation at 55 °C, the solution was vigorously vortexed for 30 s to facilitate the formation of CQAS-dsCP.

The amount of dsRNA adsorbed on the ASNP surface was quantified using a series of dilutions. The addition of various amounts of dsRNA (5, 8, 10, 12, and 14 μg) to an aqueous solution of ASNP with a constant concentration (0.51 mg/mL) resulted in a series of various ASNP-dsCP mass ratios. Each sample had a final mass of 0.51 mg of silica.

Different dilution ratios were used to incorporate 40 µg of dsCP into CQD. After adding 100 µL of Na2SO4, the samples were incubated overnight at 4 °C. The precipitate was then dissolved in 40 µL of water after the samples had been centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 g.

The NPs were confirmed using 1% agarose gel electrophoresis on the three prepared nanomaterials. The particle shape and size distribution of the three types of NPs were determined utilizing a Nanosizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern Panalytical, Inc., Westborough, MA, USA) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Hitachi HT7800 120kv, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FITC-CQAS-dsRNA-Cy3 and CQD-dsRNA-Cy3 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: DsCP was amplified with the PVYCP-F/PVYCP-R primer pair (Table S1) and synthesized with the in vitro Transcription T7 Kit (D6140; TaKaRa Bio, Inc.). During synthesis, 1 µL of Cy3-UTP was added to produce Cy3-labeled dsCP. Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was dissolved in DMSO at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. An equal volume of FITC/DMSO and a 1% solution of CQAS were mixed and stirred in the dark for 3 h. The unconnected FITC was removed by transferring the crude product to a dialysis bag. It was determined that the concentration was 0.5 mg/mL. Fluorescently labeled NPs were produced by mixing FITC-CQAS and Cy3-dsCP of equal quality. After combining with dsRNA, the fluorescence of the CQD enables it to perform synchronous tracer imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP NPs uptake and transportation in plants after infiltration spraying, or root soaking
ce:section.ce:para: The N. benthamiana plants were treated with NPs through infiltration (using a syringe to slowly infiltrate the leaves), spraying (using a sprayer to evenly spray the NPs on the leaf surface), and root soaking (the roots were soaked with the NPs). Each tobacco plant was treated with a 1 mL solution containing 80 µg of dsRNA. During processing, the leaves were kept in the same position. After 24 h, a positive control containing only ASNP, CQD, or CQAS nanomaterials without dsCP and a blank control containing the same volume of water were inoculated by friction with PVY. The leaves were lightly sprinkled with carborundum, then rubbed by hand with 100 µL of PVY PBS buffer. The inoculated leaves were collected at 6 days post-inoculation (dpi) for real-time quantitative reverse transcription PCR (qRT-PCR) and western blot experiments. The experiment was performed three times with identical biological materials. Plants were also treated with FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3 and CQD-dsCP-Cy3 NPs via infiltration, spraying, or root soaking. The transportation path of fluorescently labeled NPs in the plants was observed using confocal laser microscopy after 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and observation of the protoplasts
ce:section.ce:para: The protoplasts were prepared with the aid of an Arabidopsis protoplasm system preparation and transformation kit (PPT101; Coolaber, Beijing, China). Protoplasts were extracted from plants that were treated with FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3 NPs. The leaves were treated with 10 mL of enzymatic hydrolysate containing Cellulase R10 (PPT101; Coolaber, Beijing, China) and Macerozyme R10 (PPT101; Coolaber, Beijing, China) until they became transparent. After 30 min of incubation on ice, the protoplasts were resuspended in 5 mL of pre-cooled II-W5 solution, and the cells were observed using laser confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative real-time PCR and western blotting
ce:section.ce:para: Total RNA was extracted using the RNA Isolater Total RNA Extraction Reagent. The cDNA was reverse transcribed from 1 µg of total RNA with R323-HiScript® III RT SuperMix for qPCR (+gDNA wiper) according to the manufacturer's instructions. The β-Actin gene was used as the endogenous control. The relative level of expression of the PVY CP gene was determined utilizing the 2-ΔΔCT method. qRT-PCR was performed with the SYBR Premix Ex Taq™ kit on an ABI 7500 Fast Real-Time PCR system (Applied Biosystems, Waltham, MA, USA) using the primers listed in Table S1 to determine the relative accumulation of RNA in leaves treated in various ways. All reagents were bought from Vazyme (Nanjing, China). The total protein content of inoculated leaves was extracted, while the concentration of loading protein was maintained. SDS-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) and film transfer were performed. Primary antibodies PVY-CP (Agadia, Inc., Elkhart, IN, USA) were diluted at a concentration of 1:2000 and incubated overnight at 4 °C after the PVDF membrane had been removed and sealed for 1 h. The secondary antibodies that had been HRP-labeled were diluted at a ratio of 1:5000 and incubated for 2 h on a shaker. After TBST film washing, ECL (Supersignal West Pico Trial Kit; Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was applied to image and photograph in a chemiluminescence imaging analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The retention period of ASNP, CQD, and CQAS-dsCP NPs treated in different modes
ce:section.ce:para: DsP1, dsHC-Pro-1, dsHC-Pro-2, dsVPg-NIa, dsNIa, and dsCP were obtained through mixed prokaryotic expression and loaded with ASNP, CQD, and CQAS of equivalent quality. The following NPs were obtained: ASNP-dsRNA, CQD-dsRNA, and CQAS-dsRNA. Infiltration, spraying, and root soaking were all used to treat the N. benthamiana plants. Each tobacco plant was given a 1 mL complex containing 80 µg dsRNA. During processing, the leaves were kept in the same position. PVY was inoculated via rubbing after 24 h of NPs treatment. Carborundum was gently applied to the leaves before being rubbed by hand with 100 µL PVY PBS buffer. 7 dpi, 14 dpi, and 21 dpi after inoculation, leaf samples were collected from the entire plant, and total RNA was extracted and detected using qRT-PCR.

The miRNA sequencing analysis was conducted on three plant samples that had been treated with NPs and nanomaterials using distinct methods. The number of effective reads between 18 and 32 nucleotides was screened for quality control, and the length distribution of miRNAs in this range was calculated. The leaf samples from at least 5 N. benthamiana plants were pooled for a single biological replicate, and three biological replicates for individual groups were prepared. The samples were transmitted to a sequencing company (Shanghai Majorbio Bio-pharm Technology Company, Shanghai, China). The data were analyzed on the online platform of the Majorbio Cloud Platform (www.majorbio.com).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of PVY target sequences and prokaryotic expression of dsRNA
ce:section.ce:para: The dsRNA was designed to target PVY express genes encoding essential functional proteins, including P1, HC-pro, VPg, NIa, and CP. Using specific primers, six 500-bp fragments encoding one or two protein sequences from the P1, HC-Pro, VPg-NIa, NIa, and CP genes were cloned (Table S1). The dsP1, dsHc-Pro-1, dsHc-Pro-2, dsVpg-NIa, dsNIa, and dsCP expression vectors L4440 were constructed with KpnⅠ and BglⅡ sites and then introduced into E. coli HT115 (DE3). Bacterial dsRNA was purified utilizing cellulose (C6288, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) powder adsorption in combination with the phenol:chloroform:isoamyl alcohol method and supplemented with 3 U/µL of DNase Ⅰ and 180 U/µL of S1 Nuclease treatments. Electrophoresis was used to assess the quality of dsRNA (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the target gene silencing efficiencies of CQAS-dsCP and CQAS-dsRNA mixed
ce:section.ce:para: The N. benthamiana plants were sprayed with CQAS-dsCP and a mixture of CQAS-dsRNA (dsP1, dsHc-Pro-1, dsHc-Pro-2, dsVpg-NIa, dsNIa, and dsCP) and H2O, followed by inoculation with the same amount of PVY 24 h later. Each tobacco plant was treated with 1 mL of NPs containing 80 µg of dsCP or dsRNA. The inoculated leaves were collected at 6 dpi for qRT-PCR to detect the expression of target genes. The primers are shown in Table S1.

Pot experiment. The N. benthamiana plants were infiltrated (using a syringe to slowly infiltrate the leaves), sprayed (using a sprayer to evenly spray the NPs on the leaf surface), and root-soaked with a mixture of CQAS-dsCP and CQAS-dsRNA. Each tobacco plant was treated with a 1 mL complex containing 80 µg of dsRNA. During processing, the leaves were kept in the same position. To visually detect the virus accumulation in leaves, tobacco leaves were infiltrated with PVY-GFP, and the differences in fluorescence were observed between the CQAS-dsRNA treated leaves and controls. Additionally, Alisa Craig (AC) tomato plants were chosen for treatment with the CQAS-dsRNA mixture and PVY inoculation in three distinct ways to observe the phenotype of new leaves. These plants were treated identically to N. benthamiana plants.

Field experiment. Tobacco field experiments were conducted on May 21, 2020, in the YanJiaZhai tobacco field (ZunYi Prefecture, GuiZhou Province, China). The soil was loam, and it had excellent drainage and irrigation systems, as well as fertility levels that were above average. Following dilution, five treatment groups, including a blank control and CQAS-dsRNA NPs that had been diluted 2000 times were established in the test plot. Spray treatments were applied at three different times, during the seedling stage: two days before leaf cutting, fifteen days before transplanting, and fifteen days after transplanting. Without using insecticides, field management procedures were carried out following local technical procedures for the cultivation of high-quality tobacco. A random 5-point sample was collected, and 10 plants were surveyed at each point. A total of 50 plants were surveyed, and the number of plants and levels of major disease on roasted tobacco plants were surveyed every seven days until the plant's middle was harvested. Additionally, the growth of each treated tobacco was monitored to determine if any damage was caused by the treatments. Because tomatoes, peppers, and tobacco belong to the same family of crops, we performed a CQAS-dsRNA mixed spray resistance experiment in ShouGuang, ShanDong Province, China and selected tomatoes and peppers for the experiment. During plant development, disease index statistics were analyzed as well as the concentration of NPs in the treatment, which was identical to that used in the tobacco field.
The values are presented as the mean ± SE. All statistical analyses were conducted using GraphPad Prism 8 (GraphPad, La Jolla, CA, USA), and differences were considered significant at P < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the loading capability of NPs
ce:section.ce:para: PVY CP dsRNA was synthesized correctly using in vitro transcription according to the kit instructions, as shown in Fig. S1. In this study, it is suggested to perform in vitro transcription and extraction of dsRNA and to combine dsRNA with modified nanomaterials so that the dsRNA can enter cells with high efficiency and specificity and effectively silence target genes. Noting that cytomembranes are negatively charged due to the presence of anionic components like phospholipids, positively charged nanomaterials can be transferred to the membranes of neighboring cells via electrostatic interaction, thereby facilitating cellular uptake. It was hypothesized that dsRNAs synthesized in vitro could be specifically internalized by plant cells if they were sufficiently small and possessed the appropriate electrical properties after forming a complex with nanomaterials. The average sizes of ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP were estimated to be 30 nm, 20 nm, and 150 nm, respectively ( Fig. 1A, B, and C). Moreover, the ζ-potential of the particles were 18.9 ± 2.94 mV, 29.9 ± 2.38 mV, and 32.7 ± 2.29 mV, respectively (Fig. 1D). Positively charged spheres were synthesized by complexing ASNP, CQD, and CQAS. dsRNAs with varying mass ratios of ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP were loaded to accommodate the dsRNA payloads in the proposed nanomaterials. dsCP did not migrate from the pores in agarose gel electrophoresis after being successfully loaded with nanomaterials. The findings revealed that the maximum loading capacity of 0.51 mg ASNP was 8 μg dsCP, that of 30 mg CQD was 40 μg dsCP, and that of 30 L of 0.02% CQAS was 8 μg dsCP (Fig. 1D, E, and F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and distribution of fluorescently labeled NPs
ce:section.ce:para: It was suggested that the cell wall may have influenced the NP size selection (Palocci et al., 2017). Since all three NPs synthesized were positively charged, they are readily absorbed by negatively charged cell walls. To compare the efficiency of penetration through the cell wall, larger CQAS-dsRNA (150 nm) and smaller CQD-dsRNA (20 nm) were used.

To verify this hypothesis, FITC-CQAS and CQD were first evaluated for their ability to deliver dsCP-Cy3 into the stems and leaves. Fig. S2 demonstrates that dsRNA was successfully labeled with Cy3-UTP. The FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3 and CQD-dsCP-Cy3 NPs were applied via infiltration, spraying, or root soaking, and the fluorescence of treated leaves or stems was observed 24 h later to detect fluorescently-labeled NPs. Similar to previous reports on the internalization of NPs by plant cells, as shown in Fig. 2 and S3, FITC-CQAS and CQD delivered dsCP-Cy3 that was capable of penetrating the root epidermis, and the fluorescence was significantly stronger than that of dsCP-Cy3 alone. According to the results shown in Fig. 2, the compounds were absorbed by plants because fluorescence was observed in both infiltrated and sprayed leaves, as well as weak fluorescence in corresponding system leaves. After 24 h of root soaking with NPs, the system's leaves showed weak fluorescence. CQAS and dsCP were detected in transverse sections of the stem, indicating that NPs can be transported from roots to leaves via the phloem. Unlike pure dsRNAs, nanomaterials can bind to and protect dsRNAs. Pure dsRNAs degraded rapidly when grown in a hydroponic nutrient solution, and dsRNAs were barely detectable in the upper leaves. However, when combined with CQAS, nanomaterials carried the dsRNAs upward, significantly improving the efficiency of NPs absorption.

Other studies have demonstrated that NPs exert their effects on plant growth through either internalization into the plant cells or adhesion to the cellular walls (Serag et al., 2011). The ability of the as-prepared NPs to enter the root cells was therefore examined. Under a fluorescence microscope, the location of the NPs in root cells was observed using FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3. As shown in Fig. 3A, FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3 particles adhered to the cell walls of N. benthamiana grown in the nutrient solution after being soaked for a brief (4 h) incubation. Consequently, it was hypothesized that when higher concentrations of NPs were enriched around the cell walls, the concentration difference between the NPs inside and outside the cell walls would cause the NPs to penetrate the cell walls and infiltrate the plant tissues. These NPs were then transported away from the application site into the vascular tissues (Yang and Watts, 2005). When combined with the fluorescence observation findings of the former cross-sectional of the stem, it was concluded that the CQAS-dsCP NPs adhered to the cell wall and were internalized by the cell through the concentration difference. Then, through the phloem, they were delivered to the upper leaves.

Then, it was determined whether fluorescent signals would appear in the protoplasts following treatment with FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3. The protoplasm was observed to emit green and red fluorescent signals, while red fluorescent signals accumulated in the cytoplasm (Fig. 3B). The distribution of FITC-CQAS-dsCP-Cy3 in healthy tobacco protoplasts was observed in the protoplasts. The uptake of FITC-CQAS by plant protoplasts indicated that the nanomaterials enhanced the penetration of dsCP through the plant cell membranes (De La Torre-Roche et al., 2012). Cy3-dsRNA was successfully introduced into the leaf protoplasm when combined with FITC-CQAS, as shown by the two-color fluorescent signals that were detected in the protoplasm of the cells treated by all three delivery modalities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material- and method-dependent RNAi efficiencies enhance PVY resistance
ce:section.ce:para: Since the largest sized particles of CQAS-dsRNA NPs have been shown to be efficiently transported into system leaves and roots, it was investigated whether ASNP, CQD, and CQAS could deliver dsRNAs capable of silencing the target gene expression and affecting the virus resistance of plants. To determine whether topically applied NPs could induce gene silencing, the RNAi efficiency was calculated using two factors: the material of delivery (referred to as material-dependent RNAi efficiency) and the method of application (referred to as method-dependent RNAi efficiency), with the previous target gene PVY CP serving as an indicator.

For the material-dependent RNAi efficiency, qRT-PCR and western blotting were used to compare the difference in virus accumulation in the leaves of three NP treatments under the same application mode. ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP were found to significantly reduce the relative levels of expression of the CP gene and protein when compared to the pure dsCP treatment and material-only (ASNP, CQD, and CQAS) treatments. The materials exhibited antiviral properties, but the pure dsRNA was significantly more effective at inactivating the virus. There were significant differences in viral accumulation between the ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP infiltration treatments ( Fig. 4A). In the spraying and root soaking treatments, there were notable differences in the viral accumulation between the control, ASNP-dsCP, CQD-dsCP, and CQAS-dsCP treatment groups. Additionally, there were noticeable differences in the viral accumulation between the pure dsRNA-treated group and the NPs-treated group (Fig. 4B, C). After incorporating the material and dsRNA into NPs, it was discovered that CQD-dsCP was the most effective treatment when infiltrated. CQAS-dsCP was most effective when the treatments were sprayed, whereas CQD-dsCP was most efficient when the treatments were applied by soaking the roots.

qRT-PCR and western blotting were used to determine the method-dependent RNAi efficiency based on the differences in virus accumulation in the system leaves following infiltration, spraying, and root soaking of three NPs. ASNP-dsRNA significantly reduced virus accumulation in the systematic leaves of all three treatments when compared to the control. In addition, there were noticeable differences in viral accumulation between the three ASNP-dsRNA treatments. Comprehensive analysis revealed that root soaking with ASNP-dsCP NPs is the most effective antiviral treatment, followed by infiltration (Fig. 4D). CQD-dsCP NPs were more effective when treated by infiltration (Fig. 4E). When the roots were soaked and then sprayed, CQAS-dsCP NPs proved to be the most effective antiviral treatment (Fig. 4F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ASNP, CQD, and CQAS that delivered dsCP in different manners produce systemic protection
ce:section.ce:para: Further tests were conducted to determine whether NPs could provide systemic protection to newly emerged untreated leaves following infiltration, spraying, or root soaking treatments. During the pre-experiment, the efficiency of inhibiting the gene with pure dsRNA was compared to that of mixed dsRNA, Each tobacco was treated with CQAS NPs containing 80 µg of dsRNA and found that the same dose of mixed dsRNA (dsP1, dsHc-Pro-1, dsHc-Pro-2, dsVpg-NIa, dsNIa, and dsCP) had significantly higher suppression efficiency than that of single dsCP. Therefore, mixed dsRNAs were selected for the duration of the RNAi experiment. On day 0, plants were sprayed or infiltrated, and the treated leaves were identified. After 7 d, 14 d, and 21 d of PVY inoculation, RNA was extracted from the newly emerged, untreated leaves of these plants. From the root soaking group, leaves in the same position were collected. After 7 d of infiltration, spraying, or root soaking, respectively, it was discovered that the relative levels of PVY CP gene expression in the leaves of the ASNP-dsRNA, CQD-dsRNA, and CQAS-dsRNA groups were lower than those of the blank control group. This finding indicated that NPs could improve N. benthamiana plants' tolerance to PVY.

At 21 d, the amount of virus that had accumulated as a result of the CQAS-dsRNA treatment decreased in both the spraying and root soaking groups, whereas it dramatically increased in the infiltration group ( Fig. 5A). Antiviral efficacy in the CQD-dsRNA-injected group was high at 14 d, and virus accumulation in the systemic leaves increased significantly at 21 d. In contrast, spraying and root soaking with CQD-dsRNA could provide longer-lasting plant protection. Spraying, in particular, led to a slower accumulation of virus following treatment with CQD-dsRNA, thereby protecting plants for an extended time (Fig. 5B). When ASNP-dsRNA was applied by spraying or root soaking, virus accumulation was very low before 14 d, and the spraying group had a very significant protective effect on the systemic leaves at 21 d. However, when using the infiltration method, virus accumulation at 21 d increased significantly, and ASNP-dsRNA lost its protective effect relative to the other two methods (Fig. 5B). It was determined that the root soaking and spraying treatments could offer protection for more than 21 d, while the infiltration treatment could offer antiviral protection for at least 14 d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro expression of prokaryotes, dsRNA extraction, and the target gene silencing efficiency comparison
ce:section.ce:para: L4440-dsP1, L4440-dsHc-Pro-1, L4440-dsHc-Pro-2, L4440-dsVpg-NIa, L4440-dsNIa, and L4440-dsCP prokaryotic expression plasmids were constructed, and after double digestion by Bgl II and Kpn I, target bands of approximately 500 bp and 2624 bp were detected, respectively ( Fig. 6 A). L4440-dsP1, L4440-dsHc-Pro-1, L4440-dsHc-Pro-2, L4440-dsVpg-NIa, L4440-dsNIa, and L4440-dsCP prokaryotic expression recombinant expression plasmids were successfully constructed based on the fragment sizes and sequence analysis. The prokaryotic expression of the recombinant expression plasmid was transformed into E. coli HT115 competent cells (Fig. 6B), induced by IPTG and extracted by adsorption using cellulose powder. The outcomes demonstrated that RNase-Free DNase I and RNase digested the nucleic acid extracted from the bacterial broth containing the recombinant L4440-dsP1, L4440-dsHc-Pro-1, L4440-dsHc-Pro-2, L4440-dsVpg-NIa, L4440-dsNIa, and L4440-dsCP expression vectors. This experiment demonstrated the expression of the target dsRNA fragment (approximately 500 bp) (Fig. 6C), demonstrating that the L4440-dsRdRP1461–1774 broth successfully expressed dsRNA following induction.

The following experiment was carried out to compare the difference between targeting single genes versus multiple targets of dsRNA and to confirm that dsRNAs in different regions of the PVY genome can indeed inhibit virus particle replication within infected plants. After analyzing the patterns of expression of each target gene in response to these three treatments, it was determined that the levels of expression of viral P1, HC-Pro, VPg, NIa, and CP genes decreased to varying degrees following treatment with dsCP or dsRNA. Targeting the P1 gene resulted in a 7-fold reduction in the level of expression in the dsCP treatment group compared to the control group and a 12-fold reduction in the expression level in the mixed ds group. Targeting the HC-Pro gene resulted in a 10-fold decrease in the expression level of the mixed ds group and a 1-fold reduction in the expression level of the dsCP-treated group compared to the control group. The level of expression of the dsCP-treated group and the mixed ds group were both reduced by 2- and 3-folds, respectively, when the VPg gene was targeted in comparison to the control group. When the NIa gene was targeted, the level of expression in the dsCP-treated group was 7-fold lower than in the control group, while the level of expression in the mixed ds group was 16-fold lower. Targeting the CP gene caused the expression levels of the dsCP treatment group and the mixed ds group to be 2- and 5-fold lower, respectively, when compared to the control group (Fig. 6D). In general, multi-target viral gene degradation was more efficient and inhibited the target virus significantly more effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQAS delivery of the dsRNA mix resulted in efficient antiviral effects in tobacco, tomato, and pepper
ce:section.ce:para: Green fluorescent protein (GFP) fluorescence signal intensity was used to evaluate the differences in PVY-GFP virus accumulation in leaves before and after CQAS-dsCP and CQAS-dsRNA application ( Fig. 7A). When NP was sprayed on N. benthamiana leaves five days after inoculation, the number and size of infection zones were significantly reduced compared to the control. After 15 days of PVY inoculation in tomato, new leaves of the water-treated group started to curl and fold, whereas the curling of the leaves in the NPs-treated group was significantly less pronounced, and the CQAS-dsRNA mixture treated group did not exhibit any curling of the new leaves at 15 days (Fig. 7B). Finally, the efficacy of each CQAS-dsRNA mixture against PVY was evaluated in the field. Observing the growth of tobacco, tomato, and pepper in the field confirmed the material's safety for plants. From the initial application of CQAS-dsRNA until the harvest of tobacco, tomato, and peppers, no phytotoxic effects were observed. The growth of each plant was normal, and the treatments were safe for tobacco, tomato, and pepper within the range of the test doses. Additionally, the outcomes of field tests demonstrated that PVY could be controlled on tobacco, tomato, and pepper by diluting CQAS-dsRNA (Fig. 7C). When CQAS-dsRNA was applied to tobacco, tomato, and pepper, the average control rate was 63.99%, 64.86%, and 56.63%, respectively.

Improving the bioavailability and physical and chemical stability of RNAi-targeted agrochemicals will be crucial future endeavors. Numerous variables affect the effectiveness of RNAi (Kurreck, 2009), including the method of dsRNA introduction, the selection of gene fragments, the dose of introduction, and the length of the growth period. All three of the nanomaterials employed in this study are biocompatible, low-toxic, biodegradable, and capable of adhering to and transporting dsRNA with only slight alterations. This strategy has the potential to become a sustainable, targeted and environmentally friendly method of managing plant viral diseases. When CQAS-dsRNA is sprayed on foliage, the use of dsRNA can inhibit viral infection and reduce the damage caused by a viral disease. Consequently, this method is an environmentally friendly method for controlling viral diseases. Low-cost CQAS nanomaterials have been shown to increase the stability of dsRNA, protecting it from degradation; these NPs can be used universally and are highly effective against PVY in infected solanaceous crops.

The large-scale control of diseases, pests, and weeds in agricultural production necessitates enormous amounts of RNAs, so it is essential to lower production costs by improving the effectiveness of dsRNA manufacturing. This study demonstrates the importance of in vitro transcription and dsRNA extraction. Previous studies have shown that dsRNAs expressed in bacteria can confer resistance against Pepper mild mottle virus (PMMoV) (Tenllado et al., 2003), Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) (Konakalla et al., 2016), and Cucumber mosaic virus (CMV) (Holeva et al., 2021). They have also shown that modified nanomaterials combined with dsRNA can be used to choose the best drug delivery methods that allow the dsRNA to enter cells with high efficiency and specificity before silencing the target genes. A prokaryotic expression system employing PVY functional protein fragments was developed, allowing for the preparation of dsRNA at a low cost and with high production efficiency. Due to the frequent occurrence of multiple viral diseases during the growth of field crops, it is also possible to consider mixing multiple types of dsRNA against multiple pathogens. A prokaryotic expression system makes it possible to conveniently produce and treat a variety of viral diseases, which would significantly increase the demand for this kind of disease management. Research indicates that agribusiness is more likely to invest in the development of RNAi-based products with improved biosafety at a time when EU strategies emphasize sustainable development and substantial reductions of controversial pesticides (Mat Jalaluddin et al., 2020). Consequently, the biological safety of NPs is also an important aspect of formulation development, and a risk assessment of the formulated dsRNA pesticide should be conducted according to the specific situation prior to its application on a large scale (Taning et al., 2021).

In addition, the transport of NPs after absorption into the plant and the effect on the accumulation of endogenous small RNA in the plant were investigated. It has been reported that roots can absorb and accumulate NPs with both positive and negative charges within a certain particle size range (X.D. Sun et al., 2020; Y. Sun et al., 2020). Larue et al. demonstrated that the uptake of titanium dioxide (TiO2) NPs by wheat (Triticum aestivum) is dependent on particle size, with particles smaller than 36 nm being transferred from the roots to the leaves while particles between 40 and 140 nm remained in the roots (Larue et al., 2012). Tobacco protoplasts could internalize polystyrene nanobeads up to 1000 nm, according to Bandmann and Homann (Bandmann and Homann, 2012). This can be explained by the fact that endocytic vesicles vary in size between species. In this study, the uptake and transport pathways of NPs were observed in real-time using fluorescent labeling, and the effective uptake and upward transport of NPs under different modes of NPs delivery were confirmed. It was discovered that nanomaterials could encourage the internalization of dsRNA into plant cells when compared to the effect of gene silencing. The use of nanomedicines did have an impact on the proportion of endogenous miRNAs in plants (Fig. S4). Parker et al. utilized 32P-radiolabeled dsRNA to conduct biosafety assessments and demonstrated the process of dsRNA adsorption, degradation, and uptake by microorganisms in the soil. These findings provide methods for ecological risk assessment of emerging dsRNA pesticides (Parker, et al., 2019).

RNAi-based plant protection products are expected to possess an enhanced specificity than conventional pesticides, following proper safety assessments, and are expected to be available for practical use in the near future. Pesticides based on dsRNA usually take a longer duration to show substantial efficacy and must be evaluated after a long-term risk assessment to determine that NPs do not have adverse effects on humans and the environment. In assessing the environmental risks associated with the application of NPs, the distribution, fate, and stability of dsRNAs in the environment need to be determined following product application. It is critical to study the potential effects on non-target organisms in particular (Dietz-Pfeilstetter et al., 2021). This mode of action is highly specific due to the specific interactions of siRNAs with complementary target genes. Still, because of the partial sequence homology of dsRNA and transcription products in non-target organisms, the risk of potential effects on non-target organisms must also be taken into consideration(Christiaens et al., 2018).

In conclusion, the results demonstrated that dsRNA-NPs can be delivered to the upper leaves of plants by soaking their roots to induce RNA interference for antiviral purposes. RNAi pesticides were subsequently sprayed on tobacco seedlings to achieve maximum control during the vulnerable seedling stages. These findings will direct further investigation into the precise mechanism underlying the release of nanomaterial-loaded dsRNA in plants. The effectiveness of gene silencing caused by the dsRNA sequences of different viral target genes was also assessed, which serves as a reference point for the highly effective screening method for the PVY fragment. Before the widespread implementation and industrialization of RNAi, it might be useful for preventing and treating similar viral diseases.

This work was supported by the Green Prevention and Control Major Special Projects, China[110202101045(LS-05), 110202201020(LS-04), SCYC202008 and 2022520000240148], Science Foundation for Young Scholars of the Tobacco Research Institute of the Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, China (2021B01).
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Yubing Jiao and Jinguang Yang conceived and designed the experiments. Xiang Xu and Tingting Yu wrote this manuscript and prepared the original draft. Daoshun Zhang and Hongping Song analyzed the data. Kun Huang and Yong Wang provided detection samples in this study. Lili Shen, Ying Li, Fenglong Wang, and Songbai Zhang contributed to reagents, materials, and analysis tools. Xiang Xu and Tingting Yu contributed equally to this work. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2023.114775.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: "Endocytic pathways involved in PLGA nanoparticle uptake by grapevine cells and role of cell wall and membrane in size selection"
